Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

When the Occurrence occurs, There is, for its occurrence, no denial. (1-2)

(The Resurrection is inevitable). 'Al-Waqi'ah' (the Occurrence) refers to the Resurrection. Describing it with this word reveals its inevitable nature. Its proofs have been explained with full detail in the previous Surahs of this group, and those doubts and questions which the deniers raised regarding its possibility and occurrence have also been answered one by one. Now, it is stated: 'Remember that time when the inevitable will necessarily occur, contrary to all your baseless doubts and objections, and you will not be able to escape from it in any way.'

'There is, for its occurrence, no denial' (laysa li-waq'atiha kadhibah)—'kadhibah' is, in my view, a verbal noun (masdar) like 'aqibah' and 'afiyah'—meaning there is no room for any doubt, suspicion, or denial regarding its occurrence. If you are in the misconception that you are being warned with a false threat, there is not even a minor trace of falsehood in it. It is a real event which you must necessarily face; so if you desire success in the Hereafter, prepare to face it.

"It will bring down [some] and raise up [others]." (3)

(In the Resurrection, the Standard of Honor Will Be Faith). That is, do not remain in the misconception that the elevation you enjoy today will remain forever, and those whom you consider contemptible and low will remain in this state; rather, when the inevitable occurs, these heavens and earth will appear with new laws and rules. Today's standards of honor and dignity will be completely changed. On that Day, all honor and elevation will belong to faith and righteous deeds. Those who possess the treasure of faith and righteous deeds will be elevated and honored, and those who are deprived of this wealth will rise disgraced and humiliated. This is stated briefly here. Below, from verse 7, the detail of this bringing down and raising up is coming, which will clarify what the touchstone for this will be.

"When the earth is shaken with convulsion And the mountains are crumbled, crumbling, And they become dust dispersing." (4-6)

(The Panic of the Day of Judgment). This is a depiction of the Resurrection—showing that on that Day, the earth will be completely shaken, and these towering mountains which foolish people assume to be eternal and unshakeable will become scattered dust. From this, it is learned that on that Day, all elevations of this earth will be brought down. A convulsion will come which will shake the entire earth to make all its palaces and houses collapse—to the extent that these towering mountains will also become dust to fly in the atmosphere. This same theme is expressed in Surah Al-Haqqah as follows: 'And the earth and the mountains are lifted and crushed with a single blow - Then on that Day, the Occurrence will occur' (14-15).

(Dividing people into three groups). This is the detail of that bringing down and raising up which was mentioned in verse 3 above. It states: on that Day, you will be divided into three groups. One group will be of the companions of the right, the second of the companions of the left, and the third of the foremost. 'The companions of the right' (As-habul-Maymanah) refers, in accordance with the Qur'an's own clarification, to those whose records of deeds will be given in their right hands. Thus, in Surah Al-Haqqah, He stated: 'So as for he who is given his record in his right hand, he will say, "Here, read my record! Indeed, I was certain that I would meet my account."' (19-20). 'The companions of the left' (As-habul-Mash'amah) refers to those whose records of deeds will be given in their left hands. In Surah Al-Haqqah, their mention is in these words: 'But as for he who is given his record in his left hand, he will say, "O, I wish I had not been given my record, And had not known what is my account. O, I wish my death had been decisive! My wealth has not availed me; Gone from me is my authority."' (25-29).

'The foremost' (Sabiqoon) refers to those who excelled in accepting the truth and dedicated their lives and wealth for its service in that period when its helpers were few, and supporting it was placing oneself in danger. Thus, in Surah Al-Hadid, which is its counterpart Surah, He has clarified this reality as follows: 'Not equal among you are those who spent before the conquest [of Makkah] and fought. Those are greater in degree than those who spent afterwards and fought. But to all Allah has promised the best [reward].' (10).

"Then the companions of the right - what are the companions of the right?" (8)

The interrogative style in 'what are the companions of the right?' (ma as-habul-maymanah) is used to express grandeur and greatness, and also to express disgust; here, it is to express grandeur and greatness—meaning: what is there to say of the honor and grandeur of the companions of the right, of their eternal comfort, of their prosperity, and of their high status! How can its detail be described, and who can form a correct estimate of it! This style of expression is adopted when the reality is beyond the reach of words and human estimation. There are many examples of this in the Qur'an, and this style is also well-known in our language.

"And the companions of the left - what are the companions of the left?" (9)

Here, the same style is used in the opposite sense, i.e., to express disgust and regret—meaning: just as no one can estimate the prosperity and high status of the companions of the right, similarly, do not ask of the misfortune, disgrace, and bad end of the companions of the left! Its picture cannot be drawn in words; only those who face it will estimate it. In 'And the foremost, the foremost' (Was-sabiqoonas-sabiqoon), the second 'Sabiqoon' is the predicate (khabar), and this omission is highly eloquent. The meaning is: what is there to say of the high status of the foremost; they indeed are the foremost! When they are the foremost, who can reach their rank! They will necessarily reach that final point of human honor and excellence, and who can estimate this point of perfection in this physical world!

Correcting some misconceptions: first, this clarifies that the view is incorrect of those who assumed that this describes the arrangement of seating in the court of Allah. In His court, let alone having a concept of right, left, front, or behind is meaningless, even if it is accepted, it is accepted that there will be no place for the companions of the left in this court, neither on the left nor behind; rather, their residence will be Hell, whose explanation is coming below in this Surah and has passed in other places in the Qur'an.

Second, 'the companions of the right' (As-habul-Yameen) does not refer to general Muslims, as some have assumed; but refers specifically to those whose records of deeds will be given in their right hands as an honor, and who will be highly happy with their magnificent achievements. Among general Muslims, there are many regarding whom assuming that their records of deeds will be given in their right hands and they will be able to raise the cry of joy: 'Here, read my record!' is highly generous. As for the difference between 'the companions of the right' and 'the foremost', we have pointed to it above, and further explanation is coming.

The Reward of the Foremost

Since they hold the chief position, their rank and reward are described first. It states: these people will achieve the rank of close ones (Al-Muqarraboon). 'Al-Muqarraboon' refers, obviously, to the close ones of Allah; but their residence is described as 'the Gardens of Pleasure' (Jannat-un-Na'eem), without presenting any concept of His court itself. This shows that for the close ones of Allah, there will be gardens in accordance with their rank, in which they will be settled; below are some descriptions of their Paradise.

"A large company of the former peoples, And a few of the later peoples," (13-14)

Now, this clarifies who will be blessed with the honor of being included in this group. It states that the majority of them will be from the former peoples, and a small number from the later peoples.

What is Meant by 'Former' (Awwaloon) and 'Later' (Aakhiroon)?

By former and later, in our view, the former and later of this Ummah are meant. Above, we quoted Surah Al-Hadid, which showed: the rank of Jihad and spending of those who did so before the Conquest of Makkah is higher with Allah; the rank of those who did so later is not the same, though the promise of Allah to both is good—meaning those who come later, though they cannot reach the rank of the former in a general sense, yet through their sincerity and good deeds, the way is open for them to achieve a place among 'the companions of the right'.

From 'A large company of the former peoples' (Thullatun-minal-awwaleen), it also emerged: not all of the former will achieve the rank of close ones, but their majority will. The reason is that this rank relates not to time, but to attributes and deeds. It is possible that a person is among the former in accepting Islam, but due to his resolve, steadfastness, and sacrifices, could not achieve the rank of close ones, remaining in the rank of 'the companions of the right'.

A specific point: from 'And a few of the later peoples' (wa-qaleelun-minal-aakhiroon), it emerged: among the later peoples of this Ummah as well, such people will emerge who will achieve the honor of being included in the group of the foremost. Obviously, these will be those who remain firm on the truth in the period of trials, invite to the truth, and no matter how severe the situations become and how small their number, do not lose heart. A group of this type, as is promised in the Hadiths, will continuously be born in this Ummah in every era. These people will be among the later in terms of time, but among the former in terms of their services with Allah. This is what Lord Masih (peace be upon him) expressed: 'Many who are first will be last, and many who are last will be first.'

It should be kept in mind: although these verses relate to this Ummah, in principle, this is applicable to the nation of every prophet and messenger; thus, in another place in the Qur'an, this is expressed as a general rule: 'Then We caused to inherit the Book those We have chosen of Our servants. And among them is he who wrongs himself, and among them is he who is moderate, and among them is he who is foremost in good deeds by permission of Allah.' (Fatir: 32). If you reflect, the words are different, but it also mentions those three groups which were mentioned with 'the companions of the right', 'the companions of the left', and 'the foremost'.

The Parable of the Close Ones' Paradise

This is the parable of the close ones' Paradise. First, their seating place and their posture of sitting are depicted—showing: they will sit reclining on gold-woven and adorned thrones, facing each other. 'Mawdoonah' (gold-woven) has been interpreted differently by some, but in my view, its correct meaning is what we express with the word 'adorned' (jarrao) in our language. The ancient kings of Persia sat in their courts on such gold-woven thrones, adorned with gold, diamonds, and precious stones.

Within the word 'muttaki'een' (reclining), the meaning of cushions is implied, because cushions are necessary for reclining, and were among the accessories of thrones in ancient times. Sitting facing each other is proof: their hearts will be clean of any resentment, rivalry, envy, and malice. Those who harbor malice in their hearts sit turning their faces away from one another; but the hearts of the people of Paradise will be entirely clean, as is clarified in other places; therefore, they will sit facing one another like sincere and loving relatives and companions. 'There will circulate among them young boys of everlasting youth, With vessels, pitchers and a cup from a flowing spring - No headache will they have therefrom, nor will they be intoxicated - And fruit of what they select And the meat of fowl, from whatever they desire'—this points to the provisions of hospitality which will be provided for them. It states: in their service, young boys will circulate with cups, pitchers, and cups of pure wine.

'everlasting youth' (mukhalladoon) means they will remain always of the same age. Their status will be of permanent helpers. For services, young boys of a specific age are considered most suitable, well-mannered, and active; for this reason, Allah Almighty will keep them of the same age always, and since only an understanding helper can serve his master best, those boys assigned to anyone will remain in his service always. From the words of the Qur'an, it apparently seems: Allah Almighty will create these boys specifically for this purpose. Some are of the view: the Children of the disbelievers, who die in childhood, will be employed in the service of the people of Paradise. Although there is no such indication in the Qur'an, there is also nothing contrary to it. There is no reason why the Children of the disbelievers would enter Hell. But it is also not necessary that they are employed in this service. Allah's Paradise is vast and He is Generous. He can grant them Paradise for their innocence.

'Akwab' is the plural of 'koob'—and 'koob' and 'cup' are the same thing. 'Abareeq' is the plural of 'ibreeq', which seems to have been derived from the Persian 'ab-reez', and it is established: the Arabs took many cultural words from the non-Arabs. 'Ka's' is used for the vessel and also for the wine. 'Ma'een' is used in the Qur'an for pure and unmixed water, and also for a spring of pure wine in Paradise; here, it is in this meaning.

'No headache will they have therefrom, nor will they be intoxicated'—this wine will be such: the primary benefit of wine—pleasure—will be achieved, but it will be free from all the harmful effects of the world's wine. From the world's wine, body aches, hangovers, and headaches are suffered. The wine of Paradise will be free from these. Similarly, the greatest evil of the world's wine is that it suspends the intellect, whereas intellect is man's primary jewel, and its suspension even for a minute can lead him to various destructions. The wine of Paradise will be protected from this. 'Nazafal-rajul' means: his intellect was suspended.

'And fruit of what they select And the meat of fowl, from whatever they desire'—along with wine, this mentions other accessories: the young boys will circulate before them with fruits of their selection and the meat of those birds they desire. Among food items, these two are primary. Mentioning them is mentioning all. With 'of what they select' (mimma yatakhayyaroona) and 'from whatever they desire' (mimma yashtahoona), He showed: everyone's taste and selection will be fully considered. Fruits will be presented which they select, and meat of those birds they desire.

'And fair women with large, [beautiful] eyes, The likeness of pearls well-protected'—all pleasures of food and drink are incomplete for man without his wife, whom Allah Almighty has made his source of comfort and peace. Just as in this world, man is in need of this companion, without whom his assembly remains empty, in Paradise also his pleasure would remain incomplete if she did not share in it; therefore, Allah Almighty will give him Houris with large eyes, untouched and protected like pearls. Within these two attributes, all aspects of their apparent and hidden beauty are combined.

"As recompense for what they used to do." (24) This is that primary honor which these valiant ones will achieve. It states: whatever they receive, they will receive in return for their deeds. They will be its deserving ones, and the Generous Lord will necessarily pay them. Keep in view this aspect of human nature: the value a person places on what he has achieved as his right is not what he places on what was received by accident or as charity, even if the latter is greater than the former.

"They will not hear therein ill speech or commission of sin - Only a saying: 'Peace, peace.'" (25-26) This points to their un-altered comfort: whatever nonsense and slanders they had to hear from enemies and critics, they had heard in the world, and whatever suffering they had to bear, they had borne. There, neither will any speak nonsense, nor will any word of sin enter their ears. For them is only mercy and peace—peace from the Merciful Lord, peace from the angels, and peace from their companions! Peace in the morning and peace in the evening.

"And the companions of the right - what are the companions of the right?" (27) This describes the Paradise of the companions of the right. Above, their mention has passed with 'the companions of the right' (As-habul-Maymanah), which established that 'the companions of the right' (As-habul-Maymanah) and 'the companions of the right' (As-habul-Yameen) share the same meaning—meaning those whose records of deeds will be given in their right hands. 'what are the companions of the right?' (ma as-habul-yameen) shares the same meaning as 'what are the companions of the right?' above—implying: what is there to say of their rank! what is there to say of their grandeur! how can their comfort be described!

"Among loto-trees thornless And banana trees layered And shade extended And water poured out And fruit abundant, Neither limited nor forbidden," (28-33) This describes their Paradise's fruits, shade, and freshness.

'Among loto-trees thornless'—'sidr' means a loto-tree. In our regions, the loto-tree does not enjoy much value; because of this, some might wonder: what is this fruit of which the Qur'an has made mention. It should be kept in mind: first, not all loto-trees of every region are alike; in some regions, its fruits are highly delicious, fragrant, and beautiful. Second, this is the loto-tree of Paradise, whose mention can occur in this world only by way of comparison. Its true reality cannot be known here; only those will be familiar who achieve the honor of being included in the companions of the right. However, the Qur'an shows that this tree enjoys a specific distinction with Allah. In Surah An-Najm, He stated: 'And he certainly saw him in another descent At the Lote Tree of the Utmost Boundary - Near it is the Garden of Refuge - When there covered the Lote Tree that which covered [it]!' (An-Najm: 13-16). Under those verses, we wrote: this refers to that loto-tree which is situated at the point of connection between the physical world and the spiritual world, and near it is the Garden of Refuge where the boundaries of the spiritual world begin. Upon this tree, the Prophet (pbuh) observed those lights and manifestations which were mentioned with 'When there covered the Lote Tree that which covered [it]! His sight did not swerve, nor did it transgress.' (An-Najm: 16-17). In Hazrat Musa's (peace be upon him) initial observations of prophethood, it is also mentioned that he heard the voice of Allah from a tree and observed His lights. Although the Qur'an has not specified what tree it was, the similarity is clear, and it is possible that it was also a loto-tree.

With 'loto-trees' (sidr), the attribute 'thornless' (makhdood) is to show: this loto-tree will not be like those of the world to cause harm when anyone tries to take a fruit. They will be thornless. The people of Paradise will take their fruits as they please. 'Khaddad' means to cut off the thorns of a thorny thing. The objective is to say: like their fruits, their trees will be different from the world's loto-trees. It should be kept in mind: in this world also, those types of loto-trees which are better have fewer thorns. Thorns are numerous in wild loto-trees. In the description of the destruction of the people of Saba's garden, their loto-trees' destruction is also mentioned—showing that they became mere wild bushes. This shows that this fruit was among their beloved fruits, and its trees adorned their gardens. In its family, the loto-fruit is related to the apple.

'And banana trees layered'—'talh' means a banana tree. 'Mandhood' (layered) describes its fruits—showing that they will be layered, conjoined with one another. Their arrangement will show that the Creator has chosen them with special care for His servants' hospitality.

'And shade extended And water poured out' describes the freshness of this garden—showing that its trees will be so conjoined that no sunlight can pass; therefore, there will be shade everywhere, and water will flow in it continuously to maintain its freshness. 'And fruit abundant, Neither limited nor forbidden'—meaning those fruits mentioned above are mentioned by way of example. There will be other numerous fruits as well, and their state will be entirely different from the world's fruits. In this world, at a specific time, the fruits are taken or end of their own accord; but those trees will be evergreen, and their fruits will never end. Similarly, those trees will never face the trial of having fruits in one year but not in another. Allah Almighty will never deprive them of fruitfulness.

"And beds raised. Indeed, We have produced them [i.e., the women] in a [new] creation And made them virgins, Devoted [and] of equal age," (34-37) This describes their seating places and their wives. It will be recalled: in the mention of the foremost, the sequence was different. There are some psychological reasons for this difference, but we cannot go into details here.

It states: for their sitting, there will be raised beds, and for them will be wives whom We have produced in a specific creation. The question arises: the pronoun for wives has occurred without a referent. There is no doubt that there is no referent in words, but the context is highly clear; in Arabic, there is a proverb: 'A thing is remembered by a thing.' Here, after the mention of beds, the mention of wives is of this nature. In other places, the mention of wives has occurred along with thrones. Due to this connection, their mention here has occurred merely with a pronoun, which is highly eloquent and teaches respect in mentioning women; the fact that the mention of wives has occurred along with thrones and beds needs no reference. But for the sake of assurance, we quote some proofs. In Surah At-Tur:

'They will be reclining on thrones lined up, and We will marry them to fair women with large, [beautiful] eyes.' (At-Tur: 20). Similarly, in Surah Ya-Sin: 'They and their spouses are in shade, reclining on adorned couches.' (Ya-Sin: 56). 'Indeed, We have produced them in a [new] creation'—in praise of these Houris, He stated: We have produced them in a specific creation; therefore, their characteristics will be entirely different from the world's women. In this world, the virginity, beauty, and youth of a woman are temporary and transient.

'If it stays for a night, it does not stay for another.'

But the Houris of Paradise have been nurtured by Allah Almighty in an entirely different structure; therefore, their virginity, beauty, and youth will never face any decline. 'And made them virgins, Devoted [and] of equal age'—'fa' (and/so) is to explain that specific creation: they will always remain virgins. Their husbands, whenever they meet them, will meet them as if for the first time. 'Urub' is the plural of 'aroob'. It means a beloved and devoted wife. Obviously, when their beauty, youth, and virginity do not change, there will be no cause for them to fall from their husbands' eyes, but they will remain like a fresh flower, always desired and beloved. 'Atrab' is the plural of 'tirb'. It means of equal age, but those of taste know that its primary usage is for women; therefore, in my view, it is used in the sense of companions. In Surah An-Naba', 'playmates of equal age' (kawa'iba atraba) has occurred. The meaning is: the people of Paradise will get Houris who will all be playmates of equal age; therefore, no question of preference will arise, nor will envy and rivalry arise among these Houris. Just as they will always remain young and virgins, their husbands will remain young.

"For the companions of the right." (38)

This can relate to 'Indeed, We have produced them in a [new] creation', and can also be the predicate of an omitted subject; if we accept the first, the meaning is: We have prepared these Houris of this specific creation and these specific attributes for the companions of the right. If we accept the second, the meaning is: all these blessings mentioned above are with Us for the companions of the right. My inclination is toward the first, but whichever is chosen, there is no great difference in meaning, only a subtle difference of eloquence which those of taste can estimate; therefore, there is no need to discuss. "A large company of the former peoples, And a large company of the later peoples,"

"(39-40)

Above, in the mention of the foremost, it was stated that the majority of that group will be from the former peoples, and few from the later. Here, it is stated that of the companions of the right, there will be a group from both the former and the later. We have explained that by former and later, the former and later of this Ummah are meant; which shows that until the Resurrection, such people will continuously emerge who will be counted in the companions of the right, and will form a single group on the Day of Judgment."

"And the companions of the left - what are the companions of the left? [They will be] in scorching wind and scalding water And a shade of black smoke, Neither cool nor noble." (41-44)

The Fate of the Companions of the Left

This describes the fate of the companions of the left—stating that they will be in scorching wind and boiling water. When, being troubled by the heat, they run for water, they will receive boiling water. Their lives will pass in this. This same theme is expressed as: 'They will go around between it and scalding water, heated [to the utmost].' (Ar-Rahman: 44).

'And a shade of black smoke'—meaning they will receive no shade. Only the shade of black smoke will be there for them, which will be devoid of all those virtues which exist in shade. In shade, the primary thing is coolness, but in this shade of smoke, all those torments will be present which exist in smoke, but no coolness. Similarly, other benefits could be expected—such as being protected from the flames; but this also will not be achieved. 'Kareem' means beneficial—meaning this shade will be neither cool nor beneficial. In Surah Al-Mursalat, this is expressed as: '[Which is] no shadow, nor does it avail against the flame.' (31).

This points to those great crimes of theirs which became the cause of their bad end. The style of the discourse suggests that that Day has been made present before the people, and it is described why these unfortunate people faced this end. 'Indeed, they were, before that, indulging in affluent ease'—it states that they were, before this, i.e., in the world, wealthy and prosperous. This is mentioned not as their crime, but to show the gravity of those crimes of theirs which follow. The meaning is: Allah Almighty bestowed comfort and wealth upon them, which required that they became His grateful and obedient servants, but they showed arrogance and persisted on the greatest sin. The second objective is to highlight that bringing down and raising up which was mentioned with 'It will bring down [some] and raise up [others]' at the beginning—showing: see, those who were highest in the world, how they fell into the depth of His punishment! 'And they used to persist on the great sin' (wa-kanoo yusirroona 'alal-hinth-il-atheem)—'hinth' means sin. Its attribute 'atheem' (great) points to shirk. Shirk is, in terms of the philosophy of religion, the greatest sin, and the Qur'an has also described it as a great injustice. 'And they used to say, "When we have died and become dust and bones, are we indeed to be resurrected? Or our forefathers of old?"'—this is the mention of their second great crime: they denied the Hereafter and reward and punishment because being raised alive again after death and decay was impossible in their view; thus, when they were warned, they mocked: 'When we die and become dust and bones, will we be raised alive, and our ancestors of old, who have decayed into dust, will also be raised alive?'—meaning: this is impossible, and those who warn us of this are crazy.

"Say, 'Indeed, the former and the later are to be gathered together for the appointment of a known Day.'" (49-50)

Warning the Quraysh

This is a reply to the Quraysh—stating: 'This is the doubt you present in response to my reminding of the Hereafter, so open your ears and hear: indeed, all the former and later will be gathered together for the appointment of a known Day, and then will be raised alive.' The meaning is: those who die, do not assume that they have ended. They are being gathered in His depository, and when the appointed Day comes, they will be raised. 'are to be gathered' (la-majmoo'oona) is followed by 'ila', just as He stated in another place: 'He has decreed upon Himself mercy. He will surely assemble you for the Day of Resurrection...' (Al-An'am: 12).

"Then indeed you, O those astray, who deny, Will eat from trees of zaqqum And fill with it [your] bellies And drink on top of it from scalding water And will drink as drink the thirsty camels." (51-55) This addresses the Quraysh directly—stating: 'O those astray, who deny, do you know what will happen after being raised? After this, you will eat of the bitter fruits and leaves of the tree of Zaqqum, and will fill your stomachs with it, and then drink boiling water over it as drink the thirsty camels.' The address with these two attributes 'astray' (dalloon) and 'who deny' (mukadh-dhiboon) is in view of their two crimes mentioned. Above, their shirk and denial of the Hereafter were mentioned; in view of them, the address with 'dalloon' and 'mukadh-dhiboon' occurred—meaning: those who lost the way regarding Tawhid, and who denied the Hereafter. Above, their being 'affluent' was also mentioned. In this connection, their food in the Hereafter will be Zaqqum. They will eat its leaves and drink boiling water. 'Heem' is the plural of 'ayham'—'ayham' is that camel which has been afflicted with the disease of thirst, because of which it continues to drink water but its thirst is never quenched.

"That is their accommodation on the Day of Recompense." (56) 'Nuzul' refers, as we have been explaining, to that which is presented before a guest immediately upon his arrival. The meaning is: whose initial hospitality will be with this Zaqqum and boiling water, who can estimate what will be presented before them afterwards!

2. Subsequent Theme: Verses 57–74

Next are the proofs of the Resurrection and recompense, whose denial was mentioned above. The style of the discourse is of reproach and anger. Recite the verses. (57-74)

3. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

"We have created you, so why do you not believe?" (57) This addresses those deniers whose statement: 'When we die and become dust and bones, are we indeed to be resurrected?' was quoted above. It states: when We created you, and this is accepted by you, why do you turn away from the confirmation of the Resurrection? The meaning is: when We faced no difficulty in creating you the first time, why would We be unable to recreate you? Recreating is easier than creating the first time. This logic is strange: you accept what is more difficult, but declare impossible what is easier. After 'believe' (tusaddiqoon), its object (in reward and punishment, or in the Resurrection) is omitted due to contextual clarity.

"Have you seen that which you emit? Is it you who creates it, or are We the Creator?" (58-59)

(Arguing for the Resurrection from the creation of man). This draws attention to the creation of man—showing: if your assumption is that your planning is also involved in your creation, without which Allah Almighty cannot bring you into existence, then see how much is your share in this? It states: all that you do is that you place a drop of water in the womb of the female, and depart. Making this drop pass through different stages within the layers of darkness to bring forth a normal child from the womb, and then making him pass through the stages of childhood, maturity, youth, and old age, is whose work? Obviously, all these works occur by the power and wisdom of Allah Almighty; so the God Who can make such developments upon a drop of water, and stands in no need of anyone in His forming, if He should create you again from His earth, why would He face any difficulty!

"We have decreed death among you, and We are not to be outrun," (60) Meaning, let no one remain in the misconception that anyone can escape from Our grasp. We have spread the web of death among people, and this web has enveloped everyone. Death is necessary for everyone—small and great, rich and poor—and in this manner, We are gathering everyone for the presentation of the Resurrection. Below, in verses 83-87, this theme is coming.

"[We are competent] to replace your likenesses and produce you in what you do not know." (61) That is: when creating is in Our choice, and causing death is in Our choice, if We wish to replace you with your likenesses, why would We be unable? We are competent to create your likenesses, and to raise you alive in a world which you do not know. 'Ala' (upon/to) is an indication that 'We are not to be outrun' (ma nahnu bi-masbooqeen) is to be taken in the positive meaning, i.e., 'We are competent'. The omission of the conjoined clause due to the preposition is well-known in Arabic. This same theme is expressed in Surah Al-Ma'arij: 'So I swear by the Lord of the sunrises and sunsets that indeed We are competent To replace them with better than they; and We are not to be outrun.' (40-41).

'and produce you in what you do not know'—meaning to raise you alive in a world whose rules and laws are entirely different from this world, and you are entirely unfamiliar with them. You wonder how it is possible that after dying and decaying, by a single blowing of the Horn, the entire creation comes into existence again to be reckoned—but all this will occur, and in a world with which you are unfamiliar. 'And you have already known the first creation, so why do you not remember?' (62)—meaning: if you have not seen that world, this is no rational proof to deny it. In this world, you observe your own creation, so why do you not take warning from it: the Giver of life can recreate you, and His lordship and His wisdom require that He does so. If He did not, this world would be reduced to a plaything, and it is far below His majesty to do any vain work.

"Have you seen that which you sow? Is it you who makes it grow, or are We the grower?" (63-64)

(Arguing for the Resurrection from the provisions of lordship). After the creation of man, this draws attention to those provisions of lordship which the Generous Lord has arranged for his sustenance, in whose arrangement neither does man's planning have any share, nor any entitlement. It should be kept in mind: the blessings Allah has bestowed upon man without any entitlement have been presented in various styles as proofs of the Resurrection—showing that their being bestowed without any right is proof that one day everyone will be questioned regarding His blessings. Similarly, He has clarified that these blessings became a cause of pride for most. Upon receiving the blessings of Allah, they assumed them as their planning and capability and ancestral right, and in this pride turned away from the Hereafter, and if anyone invited them to open their eyes, they gave the reply which passed above. It states: reflect on what you sow in the earth, is it you who makes it grow, or are We the grower? Just as in the creation of a child, your share is only that you place a drop of water, in this, your share is only that you place some seeds in the earth; all subsequent stages, you observe, are performed directly by nature. He has placed within the earth the capability to nurture the seed. He has placed within the seed the capability to bring forth offshoots. He has placed within these offshoots the strength to cleave through the earth. He has given the offshoots their stalks, leaves, and fruits, which ripen to fill the farmer's store. Reflect: which of these works is performed by you or can be performed by you.

"If We willed, We could make it [debris] crumbled, and you would remain wondering," (65) That is: your helplessness is clear from the fact: if We should will, We could turn your green crop into completely crumbled debris by sending some violent wind or hail, and you would remain wondering. 'Tafakkahoon' (wondering) is used here sarcastically—meaning you would be so panicky that you would not understand what explanation to make of this accident and how to describe your loss. Below, its detail is coming.

"[Saying], 'Indeed, we are ruined, Rather, we have been deprived.'" (66-67)

That is, some would cry out: 'Indeed, we are in debt; whatever we spent, we did not receive.' Others would say: 'Rather, we have been deprived of everything; now how will we sustain our families!' In Surah Al-Qalam, a parable of the garden owners is described which presents this picture: 'Indeed, We have tried them as We tried the companions of the garden, when they swore to cut its fruit in the morning, And without making exception. So there came upon it a visitation from your Lord while they were asleep. And it became as if reaped. And they called one another at morning, "Go early to your crop if you would cut the fruit." So they set out, while they were whispering, "There shall enter it today no poor person upon you." And they went early in morning, having [the power of] prevention, strong. But when they saw it, they said, "Indeed, we are lost; Rather, we have been deprived."'

"Have you seen the water that you drink? Is it you who brought it down from the rain clouds, or are We the sender down?" (68-69)

(Pointing to the blessing of water after food). Following the provisions of food, this draws attention to the blessing of water—asking: have you ever reflected on the water you drink, did you bring it down from the rain clouds, or are We the sender down? Meaning: it is Our power, wisdom, and lordship that We evaporate the salty water of the oceans to make it rise, and then turn it clean, sweet, and pleasant to send it down upon you, which you drink, your cattle drink, and your crops are irrigated with. Tell us, who has the power to send down rain from the clouds! The experiments which scientists have made so far to send down rain are like the play of children. Dropping some cool or warm effects on a part of a cloud to make some drops fall is one thing, and creating clouds, spreading them in the atmosphere, driving them to another place through favorable winds, and watering whichever region He pleases is another.

"If We willed, We could make it salty, so why are you not grateful?" (70)

(The requirement of lordship). That is, if We willed, We could make this water so salty that it would be of no use. Meaning: when We made the salty sweet, it is not difficult for Us to make the sweet salty again. 'so why are you not grateful?' (falawla tashkuroon) is the requirement of His lordship: this obligates you to become His grateful servants, or else be prepared to face the punishment for your ungratefulness. The place of gratitude in religion has been discussed under Surah Al-Fatihah. This is that very passion under whose influence the servant takes his first step in His path, and after reaching the destination, he will also express this upon seeing the results of his efforts.

"And the last of their call will be, 'Praise to Allah, Lord of the worlds.' Have you seen the fire which you ignite? Is it you who produced its tree, or are We the producer?" (71-72)

(Pointing to the blessing of fire after water). After water, fire also holds great importance among the provisions of life, particularly for those who had to make long desert journeys where there were no settlements to obtain fire easily, nor is fire a thing to be carried, nor was any matchstick invented at that time. For such needy ones, Allah Almighty created specific stones which could be rubbed to produce fire, and of His power and wisdom, He created two such trees whose two branches could be rubbed to ignite fire. They were called Markh and 'Afar. In Surah Ya-Sin, this has occurred. It states: reflect on this, are you the creator of this life's requirement, or are We!

"We have made it a reminder and provision for the travelers." (73)

(Some aspects of the mention of fire). It states: We have made it a reminder and provision for the travelers of deserts. 'Muqween' refers to those who travel in deserts and barren lands, where obtaining fire is difficult. The referent of the feminine pronoun can be 'shajarah' (tree) and also the fire produced from it; there is no difference in meaning.

There are various aspects of 'Tadhkirah' (reminder), but we point to two primary aspects. First: this is a great sign of the Generous Lord's lordship. Lordship imposes the responsibility of reward, punishment, and the Resurrection upon man. Second: that which He has informed—that in Hell there will be fire and also Zaqqum—is a reality. Do not consider it contrary to reason to mock it. The God Who can fill the green branches of Markh and 'Afar with fire, what is difficult for Him to create Zaqqum in Hell.

In verse 60 of Surah Al-Isra', it has passed that the mockers of the Quraysh mocked: 'He says there will be fire in Hell and also Zaqqum.' Their mockery was answered from another aspect. In verses 52-53 of this Surah, since Zaqqum is mentioned, when He mentioned the tree of fire, He also drew attention: those who consider the co-existence of fire and tree in Hell impossible should take warning from this tree—showing how Allah Almighty has combined these two.

"So exalt the name of your Lord, the Most Great." (74)

(Instructing the Prophet [pbuh] to observe patience and pray). At the end, this instructed the Prophet (pbuh) to remain firm on his stance and continuously exalt his Lord. The context is: since proofs are with you but these stubborn people do not want to believe, do not care about them. Adhere to your Lord's exaltation, which is used in the sense of declaring Him pure. The meaning is: these blind worshippers of desires assume that Allah Almighty created this world merely for their enjoyment; they do not know that the Lord, the Most Great, is pure from performing any vain work. It is necessary upon Him that He brings a Day to reward His grateful servants and punish His ungrateful ones. In 'bi-ismi Rabbika', 'bi' is an indication of an encompassed meaning of seeking help—implying: exalt your Lord and ask of Him help to face these situations. 'Ism' (name) shows the reality: the medium of the servant's connection with Allah is His beautiful names alone. From their gnosis is achieved His gnosis, which is the source of all correct knowledge and deeds.

4. Subsequent Theme: Verses 75–96

Next are the concluding verses of the Surah, in which He addresses the leaders of the Quraysh to warn them: what the Qur'an is warning of is a reality; do not try to turn away from it and deny it. This is no whispering of fortune-tellers, but a noble discourse descended from His protected depository of knowledge, brought by His pure messenger. It is entirely protected from the intrusion of the devils, it is your eternal guidance and livelihood, so do not invite your own ruin by disregarding and denying it. Remember: no one is outside His grasp, and that bringing down and raising up of which you are being warned is bound to come with all its consequences. Recite the verses.

5. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

"Then, I swear by the locations of the stars," (75)

'La' (No/Then) is separate from 'Uqsimu' (I swear). Before the oath, this negation came to refute the false assumption of the addressee. Bringing negation in this manner is well-known in Arabic and the Qur'an. In Surah An-Nisa', He stated: 'But no, by your Lord, they will not believe until they make you, [O Muhammad], judge in what is disputed between them' (65)—meaning: if these hypocrites assume they have believed merely by reciting the word of faith, this assumption is entirely false. Then, He swore an oath—stating that they cannot be true believers until they make you (the Prophet) the judge in all their disputes. Similarly here, before the oath, He refuted through 'la' their assumption that the Qur'an is some whispering of fortune-tellers. After this, He swore an oath to state the greatness and sanctity of the Qur'an as divine revelation. This is a natural style of expression; because of this, it is present in every language. In our language, when you say: 'No, by God, the actual reality is this,' you use this style, and the objective is to refute the assumption or objection of the addressee before stating the actual reality. The eloquence in this style is: the opponent's objection is so absurd that the speaker does not tolerate it even for a moment; to the extent that He does not tolerate even that much delay as to explain the correct aspect first.

(A special arrangement for protecting the divine revelation). 'Mawaqi'' is the plural of 'mawqi'', which means the place of falling. Here, it is used for those stations or places from where the piercing flames (meteors) are thrown upon those devils of the Jinn who try to eavesdrop at the exalted assembly. In the Qur'an, it is clarified that the devils of the Jinn had chosen specific stations where they sat to eavesdrop. During the period of the Qur'an's sending down, to protect the divine revelation from the intrusion of the devils, Allah Almighty specifically arranged: those devils who tried to sit in these stations were struck by these projectiles. This confession has been quoted in Surah Al-Jinn through the Jinn's own tongue:

'And that we have search the heaven and found it filled with powerful guards and burning flames. And we used to sit therein in positions for hearing, but whoever listens now will find a burning flame lying in wait for him.' (8-9).

In my view, those stations which were expressed as 'maqaa'id' in Surah Al-Jinn are expressed as 'mawaqi'' in the verse under discussion. But 'maqaa'id' describes their being stations, and 'mawaqi'' describes their being the target of the projectiles. 'Nujoom' (stars) is used here in the sense of these projectiles. In Surah Al-Mulk, He stated: 'And We have certainly beautified the nearest heaven with lamps and have made them [as projectiles] for stoning devils' (Al-Mulk: 5). Under verse 1 of Surah An-Najm and verse 35 of Surah Ar-Rahman, we have commented on these projectiles; have a look.

"And indeed, it is an oath - if you could know - [most] great." (76)

A Great Parenthetical Clause

This is a great parenthetical clause between the oath and the statement sworn to. It states: just as you declare the Qur'an to be a whispering of the devils out of stubbornness, you will say regarding this oath: 'What connection does the falling of meteors have with the Jinn!', but if you could know, this reality would stand manifest: this oath contains a great testimony that the Jinn have no access to the exalted assembly, as the fortune-tellers claim. If anyone tries to reach there, nature has arranged His warning on a highly great scale. It is impossible for anyone to escape His projectiles. The meaning is: whether you know it or do not, Allah Almighty has explained in this oath a highly important secret of the universe.

"Indeed, it is a noble Qur'an In a Book protected; None touches it except the purified. [It is] a revelation from the Lord of the worlds." (77-80)

(The Qur'an is entirely protected from any touch of the devils). After the oath, this is the statement sworn to, and it has been established that the oaths in the Qur'an are sworn as witnesses. Referring to the stoning of the devils, He warned the addressees: do not assume this Qur'an to be some whispering of the devils, but this is a highly noble and exalted discourse. It is with Allah in a protected Book to which only His pure angels have access—meaning only the close angels can touch it; the Jinn and devils cannot approach it.

'a revelation from the Lord of the worlds'—meaning do not assume that this Qur'an is like that whispering of the devils which comes upon fortune-tellers. This is no whispering, but the revealed discourse of the Lord of the worlds. Its source is the Preserved Tablet, to which only the close angels have access. Its presenter is Hazrat Jibreel (peace be upon him), the chief of the angels, and the devils cannot affect him. Its sending down was upon Muhammad (pbuh), whom Allah has kept pure from every trace of deviation and error.

(Some derivations of the jurists). From 'None touches it except the purified' (la yamassuhu illa-l-mutahharoon), our jurists have derived some rules of purity which are necessary in their view when touching or reciting the Qur'an; but this verse's context clearly shows: it has no direct relation to these rules; therefore, evaluate the derivations of the jurists in the light of their proofs. This is out of our discussion; therefore, we will not address it, but will only state: those who have made the same conditions of purity for reciting or touching the Qur'an as are for prayer have went to the extreme. The Qur'an is the Book of Allah; therefore, it deserves respect, but it is also our guide to know truth, falsehood, good, and evil, and our center for derivation and reasoning. If being pure and in the state of wudu' is made necessary to touch, recite, or refer to any verse, this would be a difficulty contrary to the nature of the religion of nature. Imposing such unnatural restrictions would produce that concept of the Qur'an's respect which was described by Lord Masih: 'You were given a lamp to be placed at a high place in the house so that its light spread through the entire house, but you have covered it under a measure.'

"Then is it to this statement that you are indifferent?" (81)

Warning to Those Who Show Indifference to the Qur'an

'Idhan' (indifferent) means to show indifference and neglect. Having described the greatness of the Qur'an, He asked: 'Is this Qur'an, which your Lord has sent down with this special care for your guidance, deserving of this indifference which you are showing to it!' The meaning is: you are not so devoid of taste and insight as to have no distinction between a gem and a piece of glass. You possess the distinction, but you do not want to accept the Qur'an; because of this, you declare it a whispering of fortune-tellers to ignore it; so do so, but remember: your ignoring it will not negate the reality. Reality is after all reality, and you must face it. It would have been in your interest if you had valued it.

"And make [the reward of] your provision that you deny?" (82)

By 'provision' (rizq) here, we believe, the divine revelation, i.e., the Qur'an, is meant, which is being discussed. Describing the divine revelation as provision has occurred in previous scriptures, and we have quoted some of its references. Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him) stated: 'Man does not live by bread alone, but by every word that comes from God.' In the Qur'an, it is also expressed as life: 'and respond to Allah and to the Messenger when he calls you to that which gives you life' (Al-Anfal: 24). The meaning of the verse is: Allah has sent down His heavenly table for you to receive eternal life, but your misfortune is that you show ungratefulness and disregard to it.

"Then why, when it [i.e., the soul] at death reaches the throat, And you at that moment are looking, And We [i.e., Our angels] are nearer to him than you, but you do not see, Then why, if you are not in recompense [i.e., under government], Do you not return it, if you should be truthful?" (83-87)

No One Is Outside the Grasp of God

That is, since you are denying the Qur'an with such audacity and assuming that the reward and punishment of which it is warning you is a mere threat, and that you are under no government, nor do you have to answer before anyone for your words and deeds, why do you not save yourselves or your beloved ones from the grasp of death? When anyone's death comes, you find yourselves entirely helpless, and must hand over his soul to the angel of death; so observing this helplessness of yours, why have you assumed yourselves to be absolute and free? In verse 60 above, He stated: 'We have decreed death among you, and We are not to be outrun'—meaning: 'We have decreed death among you, and We are not to be outrun'—this very reality is portrayed here to show: no one is free. Everyone is an captive of death, and death has been decreed specifically to gather every soul to present them before God on the Day of Judgment.

'Then why, when it [i.e., the soul] at death reaches the throat'—before 'balaghati' (reaches), the subject 'Al-Nafs' (the soul) is omitted due to contextual clarity. In Surah Al-Qiyamah, the subject of 'balaghat' is similarly omitted: 'But no, when it has reached the collar bones' (Al-Qiyamah: 26). In terms of eloquence, this omission has the benefit of making the terror of the event more effective due to its implication. The reply to 'lawla' (then why) is coming in a highly effective style. 'And you at that moment are looking'—meaning this event does not occur in others' absence; but the relatives, beloved ones, and doctors of the deceased are present with him, yet the angel of death takes his soul from before their eyes, and no one can do anything. They cannot say: if they had been present, they would have held the hand of the angel of death; but all their devotion and planning go to waste. 'And We [i.e., Our angels] are nearer to him than you, but you do not see' (Wa-nahnu aqrabu ilayhi minkum walakin la tubsiroon)—pointing to the deceased. That is: you are present with him, but We are closer to him than you, yet you do not see Us. You see your doctor's hand, but do not see Our angel's hand taking his soul. 'Then why, if you are not in recompense, Do you not return it, if you should be truthful?'—this is the reply to the 'lawla' mentioned above. Since there was a distance between the condition and the reply, it was repeated. It states: if you assume you are not under any government which can seize and punish you, why do you not return this soul which Our angel takes from before your eyes? 'Madeen' means ruled and under control.

"And if he was of those brought near [to Allah], Then [for him is] rest and provision and a garden of pleasure." (88-89)

The Stages after Death

That is, do not remain in the misconception: the one who died, his story ended; but the actual stage will appear after it, which was mentioned with 'And you become [of] three kinds' (7) above—meaning his affair will not be free from three states: either he is of the close ones, of the companions of the right, or of the companions of the left. It states: if he is of the close ones, for him is eternal rest, pleasure, and the garden of pleasure. 'Rawh' means rest, and 'Rayhan' is used here in the sense of pleasure. Under Surah Ar-Rahman, we have commented on these words. 'Rayhan' primarily means flowers, but it is also used to refer to its attachments—fragrance and pleasure.

"And if he was of the companions of the right, Then [the angels will say], 'Peace for you; [you are] of the companions of the right.'" (90-91) And if he is of the companions of the right, he will receive appreciation from Allah Almighty and His angels: 'O companion of the right, for you is peace and blessing.'

'Then [the angels will say], "Peace for you; [you are] of the companions of the right."' (fa-salamun laka min as-habil-yameen)—'min' (from/of) is not used as the preposition of 'salam', as commentators have generally assumed, but is used to clarify the second-person pronoun; therefore, in my view, the correct translation is: 'O companion of the right, for you is peace.' Within this peace and blessing is summarized all that has been described of their rank.

"And if he was of the deniers, the astray, Then [for him is] accommodation of scalding water And burning in Hellfire." (92-94)

This describes the fate of the companions of the left, but their mention has occurred here through their actual crime: 'the deniers, the astray' (Al-Mukadh-dhibeenad-Dalloon), so that their crime is clarified along with their fate, and is fully applicable to the deniers and the astray of the Quraysh. Above, in verses 51-54, addressing the Quraysh, He stated: 'Then indeed you, O those astray, who deny, Will eat from trees of zaqqum... And drink on top of it from scalding water'; the same is summarized here: 'dalloon' and 'mukadh-dhiboon'—meaning those who lost the way regarding Tawhid, and who denied the Hereafter.

'Then [for him is] accommodation of scalding water And burning in Hellfire'—this shows that their first hospitality will be with boiling water. After this, they will be cast into the actual punishment of Hell.

"Indeed, this is the certain truth. So exalt the name of your Lord, the Most Great." (95-96) This is instruction of patience and steadfastness to the Prophet (pbuh), and consolation—stating: what has been described above is indeed the certain truth. There is no doubt in it; if your nation is not believing, leave them to their state, and continuously exalt your Lord, the Most Great. The context and the meaning of this exaltation have been discussed under verse 74. By the favor of Allah, the commentary of this Surah is completed. Praise belongs to Allah for His favor. Rahman Abad. 24 October, 1977 AD. 11 Dhu-l-Qa'dah, 1397 AH.
